title: Summer Of 4 Ft 2

jokes.text: D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer's signature exclamation as opening
jokes.punchline: The iconic catchphrase that signals we're watching The Simpsons
jokes.explanation: Homer's signature catchphrase - perfectly integrated character comedy that became a cultural touchstone. High quotability and rewatch value.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: It's the last day of school, Milhouse. Try to show some dignity.
jokes.setup: Milhouse acting childishly excited about sprinklers
jokes.punchline: Bart telling him to show dignity while they're both just kids
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast between Bart's pseudo-maturity and Milhouse's enthusiasm. Well-structured but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Sprinklers! Sprinklers! Sprinklers!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Milhouse, show some dignity.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: But we're kids!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: Gentlemen, start your chimes.
jokes.setup: End of school day bell about to ring
jokes.punchline: Parody of 'Gentlemen, start your engines' racing phrase
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay with good economy. The racing reference works well for the excitement of school ending.

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Gentlemen, start your engines!

dialogue.speaker: Students
dialogue.line: SCREEEECH!

jokes.text: School's out! Up yours, Krabappel!
jokes.setup: School bell rings, freedom at last
jokes.punchline: Bart's immediate rebellious outburst toward his teacher
jokes.explanation: Very much in character for Bart - the immediate rebellion is perfectly timed. Predictable but well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Eat my shorts!

jokes.text: Well, I'm glad the rest of you remembered that summer vacation starts at the end of the day, not the beginning. Quick work. How'd you know he was gonna run? We've got someone on the inside.
jokes.setup: Teacher addressing class, then talking to Willie about catching Bart
jokes.punchline: Reveals they have an informant among the students
jokes.explanation: Good subversion - what seems like a routine capture is revealed to be an organized conspiracy. The 'inside man' concept is unexpectedly dark for a school setting.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Krabappel
dialogue.line: Class, I know someone let a frog loose in the science lab. Now, who was it?

dialogue.speaker: Groundskeeper Willie
dialogue.line: I'll find the wee bampot, Mrs. Krabappel. I've got my sources.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Krabappel
dialogue.line: Sources? Willie, how will you possibly know who did it?

dialogue.speaker: Groundskeeper Willie
dialogue.line: Och, I've got an informant right there in the classroom. That's how we catch all the troublemakers.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Krabappel
dialogue.line: An informant? Who?

dialogue.speaker: Groundskeeper Willie
dialogue.line: Martin Prince. The lad tells me everything that happens in this school.

jokes.text: The leatheroleum covers were worth the extra money! Oh! You can smell the benzene! Oh, when the kids see these layouts and fonts... you're going to be the most popular girl in school!
jokes.setup: Martin excitedly examining the yearbook
jokes.punchline: His nerdy enthusiasm about toxic chemicals and typography making Lisa popular
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character work - Martin being excited about benzene fumes and thinking fonts will make someone popular is perfectly absurd nerd logic.

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: Behold! The yearbook layout utilizes a fascinating sans-serif typeface with kerning that would make Gutenberg weep!

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: And look at the photographic paper stock — it's infused with trace amounts of mercury and lead! Marvelous!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Martin, that's... actually really interesting.

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: It is, isn't it? Finally, someone who appreciates the finer points of carcinogenic materials and design principles!

jokes.text: Well, that's it. Have a nice summer, everyone. But what happened in Ford's Theatre? Was President Lincoln okay? He was fine.
jokes.setup: Teacher ending history lesson mid-assassination story
jokes.punchline: Ralph's innocent question and teacher's false reassurance about Lincoln being 'fine'
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy. Ralph's innocent concern and the teacher's casual lie about Lincoln being 'fine' after being shot is perfectly absurd and character-driven.

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: And that's how Abraham Lincoln was shot in the back of the head in Ford's Theatre.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Is he gonna be okay?

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: No, Ralph, he died.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Who died and made you boss? Mr. Estes, the publications adviser. I edited the whole thing.
jokes.setup: Student challenging Lisa's authority over yearbook signing
jokes.punchline: Lisa's literal answer to the rhetorical question about someone dying
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion of the common phrase 'who died and made you boss' with a literal death reference. Good structure and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Who died and made you the yearbook queen?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, technically, yearbook queen Sandra died in a car accident last year.

jokes.text: If you hadn't done it, some other loser would have, so quit milking it.
jokes.setup: Lisa explaining she edited the yearbook
jokes.punchline: Harsh dismissal of her accomplishment
jokes.explanation: Basic put-down humor. Functional for establishing Lisa's unpopularity but not particularly clever or memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I edited the yearbook.

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Ha-ha!

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Nerd!

jokes.text: This is Becky. She's in the second grade. Hi, Becky. Thanks for coming out.
jokes.setup: Lisa asking kids to sign her yearbook
jokes.punchline: Only a second-grader wants to sign it, and Lisa treats it like a celebrity appearance
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy showing Lisa's desperation for popularity. The formal introduction to a second-grader is perfectly awkward.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Will you sign my yearbook?

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: How about you?

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Second-grader
dialogue.line: I'll sign it!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Wow! Can I get your autograph too? And maybe a photo? You're so talented!

jokes.text: See ya. He writes the way people talk!
jokes.setup: Kid signing Lisa's yearbook with simple message
jokes.punchline: Becky's amazement at basic writing being conversational
jokes.explanation: Funny child logic - being impressed by normal conversational writing. Good economy and captures how kids view basic things as revolutionary.

dialogue.speaker: Becky
dialogue.line: Dear Lisa, you are cool. Your friend, Becky. Wow, we really connected!

jokes.text: Your daughter is named Seymour? Well, I-Yeah. I lied. It's for me.
jokes.setup: Skinner presumably asking someone to sign a yearbook
jokes.punchline: He's caught lying about it being for his daughter and admits it's for himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner moment - his social awkwardness and desperation for connection. The lie falling apart immediately is very him.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Would you sign my yearbook?

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Your yearbook? I thought it was for your daughter.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, okay, it's for me. I never got one when I was in school.

jokes.text: I don't get it. Straight A's, perfect attendance, bathroom timer- I should be the most popular girl in school.
jokes.setup: Lisa listing her academic achievements
jokes.punchline: Her complete misunderstanding of what makes someone popular, including timing bathroom breaks
jokes.explanation: Excellent Lisa character comedy. The 'bathroom timer' detail is perfectly nerdy and shows her complete disconnect from social realities.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I have a 4.2 GPA, I'm fluent in three languages, I've won numerous academic awards, and I understand the complex socioeconomic factors that shape modern society.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: So obviously, I'll be the most popular girl in school. I've scheduled my bathroom breaks for optimal social networking moments, and I've prepared a presentation on quantum mechanics to share at lunch.

jokes.text: Homer, I'm in a rhubarb of a pickle of a jam here.
jokes.setup: Ned explaining his predicament to Homer
jokes.punchline: His overly elaborate way of saying he's in trouble, mixing three food metaphors
jokes.explanation: Classic Flanders verbosity - combining multiple idioms unnecessarily. Very much his character and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, Homer, I'm afraid I'm in quite the pickle. Yes sir, I've really gotten myself into a fine kettle of fish, and now I'm in a real jam!

jokes.text: Oh, it's a corker of a case. Seems a man drove up onto a traffic island and hit a decorative rowboat full of geraniums. Now they're trying it as a maritime offense.
jokes.setup: Ned describing his jury duty case
jokes.punchline: Absurd case of treating a traffic accident as a maritime offense because it involved a decorative boat
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic - the legal system treating a car crash as a boat accident because of a decorative element. Very creative premise.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, the case was about a fender bender, but the plaintiff's lawyer argued it was a maritime offense because the defendant's car had hit a decorative boat in someone's yard.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: That's the most ridiculous thing I've ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, I voted guilty anyway. A boat's a boat, no matter how decorative it is!

jokes.text: I only get two weeks of vacation a year and you want me to spend it in your lousy beach house?
jokes.setup: Ned offering his beach house for free
jokes.punchline: Homer's immediate negative reaction to a generous offer
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer - ungrateful even when receiving free things. Very much in character but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A free beach house? Why would I want that?

jokes.text: Well, if it'll seal the deal... I'll take a look at your septic tank, see if I can get her humming again.
jokes.setup: Ned trying to make the beach house offer more appealing
jokes.punchline: Offering to fix Homer's septic tank as an incentive, treating it like a bonus feature
jokes.explanation: Good Flanders character moment - his eagerness to help extends to gross maintenance tasks. The 'get her humming' phrase adds personality.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: And if you buy today, I'll throw in a free septic tank cleaning! That's right, Homer, I'll personally ensure your waste management needs are handled with the utmost care and Christian charity!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Free septic tank cleaning? Sold!

jokes.text: Deal! See, Flanders? You give a little, you get a little.
jokes.setup: Homer accepting the deal after Ned adds the septic tank offer
jokes.punchline: Homer teaching Ned about compromise while receiving everything for free
jokes.explanation: Good irony - Homer acting like he's teaching Ned about fair dealing while getting a free vacation plus free plumbing work.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ned, let me teach you something about compromise. You give a little, I take a lot.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: But Homer, that's not what compromise means!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, it is now. Now, where's my free house, septic tank, and all the other stuff?

jokes.text: Hello, Mr. Brown Ground. What you got for me?
jokes.setup: Homer looking down at the ground
jokes.punchline: His casual greeting to the earth as if it's a person who might have something for him
jokes.explanation: Charming Homer absurdism - treating the ground like a friend who might have gifts. Good economy and unexpectedly endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey there, Earth. Got anything for me?

jokes.text: A friend- or a companion... or- I don't know- a stuffed animal.
jokes.setup: Marge suggesting Lisa bring a friend to the beach
jokes.punchline: Her awkward descent from 'friend' to 'stuffed animal' showing awareness Lisa has no friends
jokes.explanation: Good awkward parenting moment. Marge's progression from optimistic to realistic shows her awareness of Lisa's social struggles.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Why don't you bring a friend to the beach, Lisa?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I don't have any friends, Mom.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, bring... a acquaintance?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: A classmate? A schoolmate? Someone you've made eye contact with? Your stuffed animals?

jokes.text: Friends? Huh! These are my only friends- grown-up nerds like Gore Vidal. And even he's kissed more boys than I ever will.
jokes.setup: Lisa talking about her lack of friends
jokes.punchline: Her bitter comparison to Gore Vidal, implying both romantic inexperience and his sexuality
jokes.explanation: Sophisticated humor for a kids' show - referencing Gore Vidal's sexuality while showing Lisa's romantic pessimism. Dark but clever.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I have no friends. I'm like Gore Vidal - no romantic prospects and everybody thinks I'm gay.

jokes.text: Be myself? I've been myself for eight years, and it hasn't worked.
jokes.setup: Marge advising Lisa to just be herself
jokes.punchline: Lisa's logical rejection of advice that clearly hasn't worked for her
jokes.explanation: Excellent line that subverts the standard 'be yourself' advice with cold logic. Perfect Lisa voice and very quotable wisdom.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Just be yourself, Lisa.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom, I've been myself for eight years and it hasn't worked. I think it's time to try being someone else.

jokes.text: A microscope at the beach? Aah! What was I thinking?
jokes.setup: Lisa looking through her beach supplies
jokes.punchline: Her realization that bringing a microscope to the beach is socially inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lisa's nerdiness and her growing awareness of social norms. The microscope detail is perfectly her.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Why did I bring my microscope to the beach? Oh no... this is going to make me look like such a nerd.

jokes.text: Nah. Maybe you're getting stronger. Well, I have been eating more.
jokes.setup: Commenting on suitcase weight
jokes.punchline: Homer's pride in eating more as if it's exercise
jokes.explanation: Typical Homer logic - mistaking consumption for strength building. In character but not particularly memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, this suitcase weighs a ton! What did you pack?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Just my usual stuff.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: It must weigh 200 pounds!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I've been eating a lot lately. All that chewing and swallowing is basically exercise.

jokes.text: Bye-bye, job. Bye-bye, toothbrush. Bye-bye, Lisa Simpson.
jokes.setup: Family saying goodbye to home items
jokes.punchline: Lisa dramatically saying goodbye to her old identity
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from saying goodbye to objects to saying goodbye to herself. Shows Lisa's determination to change.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Goodbye, old identity. You've served me well, but it's time to move on to a new chapter of my life.

jokes.text: It's called Little Pwagmattasquarmsettport. It's known as America's scrod basket.
jokes.setup: Homer naming their destination
jokes.punchline: Impossibly complicated New England town name and fish-based tourism claim
jokes.explanation: Good parody of unpronounceable New England town names. 'America's scrod basket' is a funny tourism claim.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We're going to Squantum!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Squantum?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's a real place. Population: 3,000. And they've got the world's largest ball of twine made entirely of fish.

jokes.text: I thought Springfield was America's scrod basket. No. Springfield is America's crud bucket... at least according to Newsweek.
jokes.setup: Bart questioning the scrod basket claim
jokes.punchline: Homer revealing Springfield's much worse designation from a real magazine
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from 'scrod basket' to 'crud bucket' with the Newsweek reference adding credibility to the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, what's a scrod?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I don't know, son. But it sounds like trouble.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, the basket says 'scrod.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, that. It's just a type of fish. Anyway, forget about it. We've got bigger problems. According to this magazine, Springfield has been voted the worst place to live in America.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: The worst? What about the scrod?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Forget the scrod!

jokes.text: Wel-diddly-elcome, Simpsons. Oh, geez! He actually wrote 'diddly.' That was thoughtful.
jokes.setup: Reading Flanders' welcome note
jokes.punchline: Homer's grudging appreciation for Ned literally writing out his speech pattern
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Ned's obsessive attention to detail in writing his speech quirks, and Homer's unexpected appreciation.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dear Homer, welcome to my house. That's what I would say if I weren't such a nervous wreck around you. D'oh!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Huh? Why'd he write 'D'oh'?

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, Homer, I wrote down exactly how you talk so you'd feel at home.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Aww, that's actually kind of thoughtful, Flanders.

jokes.text: Well, duh! With what, Ned?
jokes.setup: Presumably reading an obvious instruction note
jokes.punchline: Homer arguing with absent Ned about unclear instructions
jokes.explanation: Typical Homer frustration with instructions. The 'with what' complaint is very him but not particularly memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Okay, let's see... 'Assembly required.' Well, that's pretty clear.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh wait, there's more. 'Some assembly required.' Okay, SOME assembly. That narrows it down.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hmm, 'Ned, you were supposed to leave clearer instructions!' Well where are you now, Neddy? Huh?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's what I thought. You're not here to defend yourself, you coward!

jokes.text: Well, it's kind of funny. With all the craziness and confusion and meshuggaas of packing, I forgot to pack!
jokes.setup: Marge explaining why she doesn't have a swimsuit
jokes.punchline: Her use of Yiddish 'meshuggaas' and the irony of forgetting to pack while packing
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay and irony. The Yiddish word adds character but overall fairly standard sitcom humor.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I don't have a swimsuit because in all the meshuggaas of packing, I forgot to pack a swimsuit.

jokes.text: I forgot my swimsuit, too, but I improvised. Hello.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining his swimsuit solution
jokes.punchline: His inappropriate 'improvised' swimwear and flirtatious greeting
jokes.explanation: Visual gag of Homer in inappropriate beach attire. The flirty 'hello' adds to the uncomfortable humor.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I don't need a swimsuit. I'll just wear my underwear!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey there, beautiful!

jokes.text: It has a starfish on the fanny, and it comes with its own pail.
jokes.setup: Describing a child's swimsuit to Lisa
jokes.punchline: The overly childish features that would make Lisa look even younger
jokes.explanation: Standard joke about embarrassingly childish clothing options. The pail detail adds to the infantilizing effect.

dialogue.speaker: Store Clerk
dialogue.line: This one has little duckies on it and puffed sleeves. It'll make you look like you're five years old.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'm eight.

dialogue.speaker: Store Clerk
dialogue.line: Well, this suit will fix that.

jokes.text: Last one in is a yearbook editor! Full speed ahead! Prepare to dive! Dive!
jokes.setup: Bart running toward the water
jokes.punchline: Using 'yearbook editor' as an insult while giving military diving commands
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Lisa's yearbook work, now used as an insult. The military commands add to Bart's dramatic flair.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Eat my shorts, you yearbook editor!

jokes.text: It's low tide, boys.
jokes.setup: Bart dramatically diving toward the ocean
jokes.punchline: Revealing there's no water to dive into
jokes.explanation: Classic physical comedy setup and payoff. Good timing with Bart's dramatic preparation followed by the anticlimactic reality.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Cowabunga!

dialogue.speaker: Bystander
dialogue.line: He's diving into the ocean!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: *crash*

dialogue.speaker: Bystander
dialogue.line: Wait... where's the water?

jokes.text: No, I can't. That's the old, nerdy Lisa.
jokes.setup: Lisa seeing a library and being tempted
jokes.punchline: Her internal struggle to resist her natural intellectual impulses
jokes.explanation: Good internal conflict showing Lisa fighting her true nature. The 'old, nerdy Lisa' phrasing is effective.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: A library! Oh, how I've missed the musty smell of knowledge...

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: No, Lisa. You're cool now. Cool kids don't go to libraries.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But the Dewey Decimal System is calling to me...

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Resist, Lisa. Think of Bart. Think of... popularity.

jokes.text: Lisa, read about my adventures in the South Seas... and make me live again. We've got periodicals on microfiche.
jokes.setup: Lisa being tempted by the library
jokes.punchline: The overly dramatic literary plea followed by mundane technical offer
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor - books literally calling to Lisa, then the anticlimatic microfiche offer. Shows her intellectual temptation.

dialogue.speaker: Librarian
dialogue.line: Come to the library, Lisa. We have so much to offer — endless worlds of imagination, knowledge that will expand your mind beyond measure, the very essence of human enlightenment at your fingertips!

dialogue.speaker: Librarian
dialogue.line: Plus, we just got a new microfiche machine.

jokes.text: Won't you join our tea party? It would be ever so- Don't do it, Lisa! It's a trick! Run! Run!
jokes.setup: More literary temptation from books
jokes.punchline: Lisa's internal voice warning herself away from intellectual activities
jokes.explanation: Excellent internal conflict visualization. The tea party invitation is perfectly nerdy, and Lisa's self-warning is dramatically effective.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: No, Lisa, no. You'll read just one page...

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: ...and then you'll be up all night, wide awake, expanding your mind.

jokes.text: So he goes, like- I can totally hear him going that. Only kids are that incoherent!
jokes.setup: Lisa overhearing teenagers talking
jokes.punchline: Her recognition that incoherent speech patterns signal actual teenagers
jokes.explanation: Smart observational humor about teen speech patterns. Lisa's analytical approach to finding kids her age is very her.

dialogue.speaker: Cool Kid 1
dialogue.line: Like, yo, that's totally tubular, dude.

dialogue.speaker: Cool Kid 2
dialogue.line: Fer sure, fer sure. Radical!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Wait... they're actually teenagers. Real teenagers talk like that.

jokes.text: Like, you know, whatever.
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to fit in with cool kids
jokes.punchline: Her awkward attempt at casual teen speech
jokes.explanation: Standard fish-out-of-water humor. Lisa's stilted delivery of teen-speak is predictable but in character.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Hey guys, what's up? I'm just hanging out, being all... chill and whatnot. Radical, am I right? That's still cool, right?

dialogue.speaker: Cool Kid 1
dialogue.line: Who is this nerd?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'm Lisa Simpson. I'm down with the kids. Yo, fo' shizzle.

jokes.text: A compliment! Scanning for sarcasm. It's clean. Go!
jokes.setup: Someone compliments Lisa's hat
jokes.punchline: Her analytical process for determining if the compliment is genuine
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Lisa character work - her systematic analysis of social interactions like a computer program. Highly quotable and perfectly her.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, let me analyze this compliment. First, I examine their facial microexpressions for signs of sarcasm. Next, I cross-reference their historical compliment patterns against their current tone of voice. I calculate the probability of sincerity using a complex algorithm that factors in their social status relative to mine, the time of day, and recent hat-related discourse in our peer group. After running these diagnostics, I can confidently say... I have no idea if you're being genuine, and it's exhausting.

jokes.text: Yeah. And you just know they're using them, like, five minutes later down at the station.
jokes.setup: Kids talking about cops confiscating skateboards
jokes.punchline: Imagining police officers secretly using the confiscated skateboards
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about authority figures. The image of cops secretly skateboarding is amusing.

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: Yeah, and then they use 'em themselves!

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse Van Houten
dialogue.line: Can you imagine? Cops on skateboards?

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: Late at night, cruisin' around the station...

dialogue.speaker: Nelson Muntz
dialogue.line: Doing tricks and stuff. Ha-ha!

jokes.text: Me? Unlikely. My gooney brother's always going to libraries. I usually hang out in front.
jokes.setup: Being asked if she's into books
jokes.punchline: Lisa denying her love of books and calling Bart 'gooney' for library visits
jokes.explanation: Excellent character irony - Lisa calling Bart the library nerd when it's obviously her. The role reversal is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Into books? No, I'm not really into books.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Only a gooney would spend all their time at the library like Bart does.

jokes.text: Well, it beats doin' stuff. Yeah. Stuff sucks.
jokes.setup: Discussion about hanging out
jokes.punchline: The anti-motivation philosophy and Lisa's agreement
jokes.explanation: Good teen slacker philosophy. Lisa agreeing that 'stuff sucks' is mildly funny character development.

dialogue.speaker: Cool kid
dialogue.line: Hey, want to hang out?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Sure, what do you want to do?

dialogue.speaker: Cool kid
dialogue.line: I dunno. Nothing motivates me. I'm too cool to try.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's the worst philosophy I've ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: Cool kid
dialogue.line: Yeah, it really sucks.

jokes.text: That's very, um, rad of you.
jokes.setup: Someone offering to let Lisa use their skateboard
jokes.punchline: Lisa's awkward use of outdated slang
jokes.explanation: Standard out-of-touch adult trying to be cool humor. The hesitation before 'rad' shows Lisa's uncertainty.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey Lisa, you can use my skateboard if you want.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh, that would be totally rad, Bart. I'm gonna catch some gnarly air and do a sick kickflip.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, Lisa... nobody says that anymore.

jokes.text: And she looks like Blossom.
jokes.setup: Milhouse observing Lisa's new look
jokes.punchline: Reference to the 90s TV character known for her quirky hat style
jokes.explanation: Good 90s pop culture reference. Comparing Lisa to Blossom works both visually and culturally for the era.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Wow, Lisa! You look just like Clarissa from Clarissa Explains It All!

jokes.text: That kid, like, tries too hard. Sh'yeah! The whole thing smacks of effort, man.
jokes.setup: Cool kids watching Bart's failed attempt to impress them
jokes.punchline: Criticizing someone for trying hard, as if effort itself is uncool
jokes.explanation: Excellent satire of teen coolness culture where trying itself is seen as uncool. 'Smacks of effort' is particularly well-written.

dialogue.speaker: Cool Kid 1
dialogue.line: Man, he's trying way too hard.

dialogue.speaker: Cool Kid 2
dialogue.line: Yeah, trying is, like, totally uncool.

jokes.text: That's my dorky little brother, Bart- holomew.
jokes.setup: Cool kids criticizing Bart
jokes.punchline: Lisa disowning Bart and making his name sound even nerdier
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lisa's willingness to sacrifice family for acceptance. The 'Bartholomew' pronunciation is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Cool Kid 1
dialogue.line: Bart, you're a total loser.

dialogue.speaker: Cool Kid 2
dialogue.line: Yeah, nobody likes you.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, but I have to disown you. From this moment on, your name is Barclay.

jokes.text: Who does he think he is with that slingshot in his back pocket? Dennis the Menace? Mr. Wilson or something.
jokes.setup: Lisa criticizing Bart's appearance to fit in
jokes.punchline: Her confused reference mixing up Dennis the Menace with his victim Mr. Wilson
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lisa's awkward attempt to criticize pop culture references. The Dennis/Mr. Wilson confusion is realistic.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, you can't keep dressing like that if you want to fit in at school.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What's wrong with how I dress?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You look like Dennis the Menace. You know, that boy who's always tormenting poor Mr. Wilson... or wait, is Mr. Wilson the one causing all the trouble?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Lisa, you have no idea what you're talking about.

jokes.text: Captain of the football team. He's a dreamboat. Don't wait up, Marge.
jokes.setup: Homer playing Mystery Date board game
jokes.punchline: His enthusiastic reaction to getting the good date and flirting with Marge
jokes.explanation: Funny visual of Homer getting into a children's dating game. His flirtation with Marge about his 'date' is charming.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh boy, oh boy, oh boy! A dinner date! Yes!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, look at me. I'm a dreamboat.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you're playing a children's game.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Shh, shh. The mystery man has spoken.

jokes.text: You got the dud! Hey, he looks just like you, Poindexter!
jokes.setup: Milhouse getting the bad result in Mystery Date
jokes.punchline: Homer pointing out the resemblance between Milhouse and the 'dud' card
jokes.explanation: Standard visual gag about Milhouse's nerdy appearance. Homer's casual cruelty to a child is typical.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey Milhouse, that guy looks just like you!

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: That's the 'dud' card...

jokes.text: Don't be afraid, little friend. You'll be the most popular crab on the beach.
jokes.setup: Lisa talking to the hermit crab
jokes.punchline: Her projection of her own popularity desires onto the crab
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of the hermit crab metaphor. Lisa's projection of her own hopes onto the crab is touching and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mr. Pinchy, don't you ever get lonely out here all by yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You know, I bet if you got out more, went to parties, made some friends, you'd be so much happier.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Everyone would want to be around you. You're special, Mr. Pinchy. You just need to believe in yourself.

jokes.text: No. No, I heard it on Baywatch.
jokes.setup: Being asked if she learned 'crustacean' from a teacher
jokes.punchline: Lisa's lie about learning scientific vocabulary from a trashy TV show
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Lisa's continued attempt to hide her intelligence. Baywatch is the perfect anti-intellectual reference.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: No, I learned it from watching 'The Itchy & Scratchy Show.' They had a whole episode about a giant enemy crab.

jokes.text: Let me have one of those porno magazines, large box of condoms... a bottle of Old Harper, a couple of those panty shields... and some illegal fireworks... and one of those disposable enemas- no, make it two.
jokes.setup: Bart trying to act casual about buying fireworks
jokes.punchline: His absurd list of adult items delivered with fake confidence, ending with enemas
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation comedy - Bart's list gets progressively more absurd and inappropriate. The enema finale is perfectly unexpected.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, uh, I'll take a pack of cigarettes, some beer, and... enemas.

dialogue.speaker: Store Clerk
dialogue.line: Enemas?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, you know, for my... back problems.

jokes.text: Celebrate the independence of your nation by blowing up a small part of it.
jokes.setup: Clerk selling fireworks for July 4th
jokes.punchline: The ironic description of patriotic celebration as destruction
jokes.explanation: Excellent observational humor about the contradiction in July 4th celebrations. Very quotable and well-crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Store Clerk
dialogue.line: Here we have the 'Patriot' — it explodes in mid-air, rains fire down on your neighborhood, and destroys everything in its path.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Sold!

jokes.text: Not like my mom. She'd be butting in with Rice Krispies squares and Tang.
jokes.setup: Comparing different parenting styles
jokes.punchline: The specific mention of wholesome but uncool mom snacks
jokes.explanation: Good specific details about uncool mom behavior. Rice Krispies squares and Tang are perfectly chosen examples.

dialogue.speaker: Cool Kid
dialogue.line: Yeah, my mom packs me Capri Suns and Dunkaroos.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: My mom makes me whole wheat sandwiches with carrot sticks.

dialogue.speaker: Cool Kid
dialogue.line: Dude, that's so lame. No wonder you're not cool.

jokes.text: He woke me up at dawn to take a loyalty oath.
jokes.setup: Kid complaining about patriotic dad
jokes.punchline: The extreme and absurd level of July 4th patriotism
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor about over-the-top patriotism. The 'dawn loyalty oath' is a funny specific detail.

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: My dad's like, the most patriotic guy ever. On the Fourth of July, he goes completely insane.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: USA! USA! USA!

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: He wrapped the entire house in the American flag, shot off fireworks from the roof, and made us all wear stars and stripes outfits to the grocery store.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: If you're not willing to look ridiculous for your country, do you even love America?

jokes.text: My mom's French, so we only celebrate Bastille Day.
jokes.setup: Discussion of family holiday traditions
jokes.punchline: The casual mention of celebrating French instead of American independence
jokes.explanation: Mild humor about alternative holiday celebrations. Works as character background but not particularly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: We celebrate French independence instead of American.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? Why would we do that?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Because it's cooler.

jokes.text: This baby's sure to kill something.
jokes.setup: Homer with large firework
jokes.punchline: His casual acceptance that his firework will probably cause death
jokes.explanation: Dark Homer humor about dangerous fireworks. His casual attitude toward potential death is very him.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, here goes nothing. This'll probably kill me.

jokes.text: D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer's firework plan going wrong
jokes.punchline: His signature exclamation of frustration
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer catchphrase at a perfect moment of failure. Always works and always quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: The beer!
jokes.setup: Homer's firework disaster
jokes.punchline: His priorities focusing on saving alcohol rather than safety
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer priorities - worried about beer instead of personal safety during a fireworks accident.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The fireworks! They're exploding everywhere!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, we need to get the children to safety!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Safety? What about my beer?!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Somebody help me save the Duff!

jokes.text: Oh! This is the worst Fourth of July ever. I hate America.
jokes.setup: Homer's failed fireworks celebration
jokes.punchline: His dramatic escalation from personal disappointment to hating the entire country
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from personal failure to national rejection. Very Homer to blame America for his own incompetence.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, the fireworks were a bust. I guess I'm just a failure.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know what? This whole Independence Day thing is stupid anyway. I hate this country!

jokes.text: Don't have a cow, man! See? That's my expression!
jokes.setup: Bart using his catchphrase
jokes.punchline: His possessive claim over what's become a cultural reference
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor about Bart's catchphrase becoming cultural property. His defensive ownership is funny character work.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Eat my shorts!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey, that's MY catchphrase! I should be getting royalties on that!

jokes.text: Oh. You haven't said that in four years. Let Lisa have it.
jokes.setup: Bart claiming ownership of his catchphrase
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's practical observation about the phrase's dormancy
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-commentary on catchphrase fatigue. Milhouse's logic about intellectual property abandonment is sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ay caramba is MY catchphrase!

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Yeah, but you haven't said it in like three years.

jokes.text: Ay, caramba!
jokes.setup: Bart's reaction to Milhouse standing up to him
jokes.punchline: His other signature catchphrase used in surprise
jokes.explanation: Another Bart catchphrase, well-timed for his surprise at Milhouse's assertiveness. Less iconic than 'Don't have a cow.'

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ay caramba!

jokes.text: I'm dizzy! I'm nauseous! Oh, but I'm popular!
jokes.setup: Lisa presumably after an intense ride or activity
jokes.punchline: Her willingness to endure physical discomfort for social acceptance
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy showing Lisa's priorities. The escalation from physical symptoms to joy is well-structured.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I can't feel my legs, but at least I'm cool now.

jokes.text: Miss Perfect Attendance... Grammar Rodeo head buckaroo, the French table. Ooh-la-la!
jokes.setup: Bart reading from Lisa's yearbook to embarrass her
jokes.punchline: The revelation of Lisa's nerdy achievements, ending with mocking French
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Lisa's earlier academic pride, now used to humiliate her. 'Grammar Rodeo head buckaroo' is a particularly funny achievement.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Listen to this: 'Lisa Simpson, Most Likely to Succeed.' Oh, and look at this picture - it says 'Lisa Simpson, Science Club President, Math Team Captain, Future Nobel Prize Winner.'

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, give that back!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait, there's more. It says here you speak French fluently. Bonjour, Lisa!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's not funny.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Au contraire, mon soeur. It's hilarious.

jokes.text: I know exactly who I am. I am the sister of a rotten, jealous, mean, little sneak! You cost me my only friends! You've ruined my life!
jokes.setup: Bart claiming his stunt taught Lisa to be herself
jokes.punchline: Lisa's furious self-identification through her relationship to Bart
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lisa's anger and self-awareness. The escalation from 'rotten' to 'ruined my life' is effective.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'm not myself because of you! I'm myself IN SPITE of you!

jokes.text: Look, we don't care who you were... and you can't fake the kind of good person that you are.
jokes.setup: Lisa's expectation of rejection after being exposed as a nerd
jokes.punchline: Erin's acceptance and recognition of Lisa's genuine character
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment but more heartwarming than funny. The 'can't fake goodness' observation is wise but not particularly comedic.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I guess everyone's going to hate me now that they know I'm a nerd.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Are you kidding? That's so cool! I think you're really smart and funny and nice.

jokes.text: Sweet merciful crap.! My car.!
jokes.setup: Homer discovering what the kids did to his car
jokes.punchline: His unique profanity and dismay at the car's condition
jokes.explanation: Good Homer reaction with his characteristic creative profanity. 'Sweet merciful crap' is a nice Homer-ism.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: The polite thing to do would've been to clean the shells first.
jokes.setup: Homer's car covered in shells and sea life
jokes.punchline: His focus on etiquette rather than the destruction itself
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer logic - focusing on the courtesy aspect of vandalism rather than the vandalism itself. Perfect character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh no, my car! All covered in shells and sea life!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But the real tragedy is I didn't use a coaster.

tmdb_overview: On a family vacation, Lisa tries on a new identity to fit in with the other kids at the beach. Jealous, Bart reveals her true nerdiness to the group.
insight: Lisa's reinvention plot carries a middling episode through character comedy over genuine laughs.
